1.title: The letter to the Romans
1.text: Paul wrote this letter to a church he had not yet visited, in Rome, the capital of the empire. It is the longest and most systematic of his letters, and it shaped Christian theology over the centuries.

1.question: How does Paul describe himself in the greeting to the Romans?
options: A servant of Jesus Christ, called to be an apostle

1.question: What did Paul desire in visiting the Romans?
options: To impart some spiritual gift to strengthen them

1.template: for it is the {0} of God unto salvation to every one that believeth;

1.title: The just shall live by faith
1.text: Verse 17 — 'the just shall live by faith', quoted from Habakkuk — is the heart of Romans and of the gospel according to Paul. Centuries later, this phrase was decisive in the life of reformers such as Martin Luther.

1.template: And changed the {0} of the uncorruptible God into an image

1.question: What did men do in exchanging the truth of God for a lie?
options: They worshipped and served the creature more than the Creator
They built a temple to God
They kept the law of Moses

1.question: What does Paul say of those who practice such evils, knowing God's judgment?
options: Not only do them, but also approve those who do them
They seek pardon in the temple

pairs.right: Servant and apostle, author of the letter to the Romans
Power of God for the salvation of the believer
Shall live by faith
Revealed against the ungodliness of men

2.title: The judge who condemns himself
2.text: Paul turns the argument against those who judge others: by condemning another's sin and doing the same, a person condemns himself. No one escapes God's judgment by thinking himself better.

2.template: the goodness of God leadeth thee to {0}?

2.question: What does Paul affirm about God's judgment?
options: With God there is no respect of persons

2.question: Who are just before God, according to Paul?
options: Not the hearers of the law, but the doers of it

2.question: What inconsistency does Paul point out in those who boast of teaching the law?
options: He teaches others, but does not teach himself

2.template: circumcision verily profiteth, if thou keep the {0}:

2.question: Who is truly a Jew, according to Paul?
options: He who is one inwardly, with circumcision of the heart
He who is one outwardly in the flesh

pairs.right: Leads to repentance
Are justified, not the mere hearers
Does not exist with God
One who is so inwardly, in the heart

3.template: let God be true, but every man a {0};

3.question: What slander does Paul reject, calling the condemnation of those who utter it just?
options: Let us do evil that good may come

3.title: There is none righteous
3.text: To prove that all have sinned, Paul strings together a series of Old Testament quotations — chiefly from the Psalms and Isaiah. Together they paint a dark portrait of humanity without God.

3.template: There is none {0}, no, not one:

3.question: Where does Paul draw the series of quotations on the sinful condition of all?
options: From the psalms and prophets of the Old Testament

3.template: For all have {0}, and come short of the glory of God;

3.title: Justified by grace
3.text: Paul presents Christ as a propitiation, a word that evoked the sacrifice of the Day of Atonement in the temple. Justification is free: it comes by grace and by the redemption in Christ, not by the merit of the believer.

3.question: How is a man justified, Paul concludes?
options: By faith, without the deeds of the law
By the deeds of the law

pairs.right: Have sinned and come short of the glory of God
Free, by grace and the redemption in Christ
Excluded by the law of faith
Is God of both Jews and Gentiles

4.title: Abraham, father of faith
4.text: Paul appeals to Abraham, the great patriarch, to show that justification has always been by faith. Abraham believed before the law existed and before he was circumcised, becoming the father of all who believe, Jews and Gentiles.

4.template: it was counted unto him for {0}.

4.question: Whom does David declare blessed?
options: The one whose iniquities are forgiven and whose sins are covered
The one who keeps all the law

4.question: Why was Abraham's faith counted as righteousness before he was circumcised?
options: To be the father of all who believe, circumcised or not

4.template: I have made thee a father of many {0},

4.question: What did Abraham not consider when believing God's promise?
options: His own body now dead and the barren womb of Sara

4.title: Faith against hope
4.text: Abraham believed the promise of a son even though he was nearly a hundred years old and Sara's womb was barren. Paul presents him as an example of one who does not waver at God's promise through unbelief, but is strengthened in faith.

4.question: Why was Abraham's faith also written for our sake, according to Paul?
options: Because righteousness will be imputed to us who believe in him who raised Jesus
Because we must imitate his journeys

pairs.right: Believed God and it was counted to him for righteousness
Declared blessed the one forgiven without works
Given by the righteousness of faith, not the law
Had a barren womb when the promise came

5.title: Peace with God
5.text: From here Paul describes the fruits of justification: peace with God, access to grace, and a hope that does not disappoint. Even tribulations become a reason to glory, for they produce patience, experience and hope.

5.template: while we were yet {0}, Christ died for us.

5.question: How did sin and death enter the world, according to Paul?

5.title: Adam and Christ
5.text: Paul calls Adam 'the figure of him that was to come', drawing a central parallel: by one man came condemnation to all; by another, Christ, comes grace and justification. Where sin abounded, grace did much more abound.

5.template: so by the {0} of one shall many be made righteous.

pairs.right: Brings peace with God
Produces patience, experience and hope
By him sin and death entered the world
By him grace and justification abounded

6.title: Baptized into Christ's death
6.text: Paul uses baptism as an image of death and resurrection: being buried in the water represents dying to sin, and rising from it, walking in newness of life. Grace is not a license to sin, but freedom from sin.

6.template: Therefore we are buried with him by {0} into death:

6.question: What happens to the 'old man', according to Paul?
options: He is crucified with Christ, that the body of sin might be destroyed
He is strengthened by the law
He remains a slave of the flesh
He was buried in the temple

6.question: What does Paul ask to be presented to God?
options: Your members as instruments of righteousness

6.title: The wages and the gift
6.text: The final verse sums up the chapter in a striking contrast: sin pays a wage, and that wage is death; but God offers a free gift, eternal life in Christ Jesus.

6.template: For the {0} of sin is death;

pairs.right: Image of dying and rising with Christ
Crucified with Christ
Death
Eternal life in Christ Jesus

7.title: The marriage analogy
7.text: Paul compares the bond to the law to a marriage: just as the husband's death frees the wife, death to the law (by the body of Christ) frees the believer to belong to another — to the risen Christ himself.

7.question: What analogy does Paul use to explain freedom from the law?
options: The wife is freed from the law of her husband when he dies
A servant freed from his master
A debtor who pays the debt

7.template: the law had said, Thou shalt not {0}.
Wherefore the law is {0}, and the commandment holy,

7.question: What inner conflict does Paul describe in this chapter?
options: He does not do the good he wills, but the evil he hates

7.question: What does Paul say dwells in his flesh?
options: No good thing; the will is present, but he cannot perform the good

7.template: who shall deliver me from the body of this {0}?

8.title: No condemnation
8.text: Romans 8 is the high point of the letter and one of the most beloved chapters in the Bible. It opens with the great declaration that there is no condemnation for those who are in Christ, and celebrates life led by the Spirit.

8.template: now no {0} to them which are in Christ Jesus,
to be spiritually minded is life and {0}.

8.question: What happens to those who, by the Spirit, mortify the deeds of the body?

8.template: ye have received the Spirit of {0}, whereby we cry, Abba, Father.

8.title: Abba, Father
8.text: 'Abba' is an Aramaic word meaning Father, intimate and affectionate in tone. Paul says the Spirit of adoption makes believers cry 'Abba, Father', witnessing that they are children of God and heirs with Christ.

8.question: What does Paul say all creation does, awaiting redemption?
options: It groans and travails in pain together until now

8.template: maketh intercession for us with {0} which cannot be uttered.
all things work together for {0} to them that love God,

8.question: What question does Paul ask to show the believer's security?
options: If God be for us, who can be against us?

8.question: What does Paul affirm that NOTHING can do to the believer?
options: Separate him from the love of Christ
Make him a slave of the law

8.title: More than conquerors
8.text: Paul closes the chapter with one of the most famous passages in the Bible: we are more than conquerors through him that loved us, and nothing — neither death, nor life, nor height, nor depth — can separate us from the love of God in Christ.

pairs.left: The law of the Spirit of life
pairs.right: Frees from the law of sin and death
Makes us cry: Abba, Father
Groans awaiting redemption
Nothing can separate us from it

9.title: Israel's privileges
9.text: Paul sorrowfully lists all that belonged to Israel: the adoption, the glory, the covenants, the law, the worship and the promises. It was from this people that, according to the flesh, Christ himself would come.

9.question: What privileges does Paul list as belonging to the Israelites?
options: The adoption, the glory, the covenants and the promises
The riches, the throne and the army
The wisdom, the strength and the fame
The prophets, the kings and the judges

9.template: In {0} shall thy seed be called.

9.title: Jacob and Esau
9.text: Paul recalls that God chose Jacob over Esau before they were born or had done good or evil, to show that God's purpose does not depend on works but on his election. It is one of the central texts in the debate about grace.

9.template: {0} have I loved, but Esau have I hated.

9.question: What does Paul conclude about God's mercy, citing Moses and Pharaoh?
options: It depends on God, who has mercy on whom he will

9.template: Hath not the potter power over the {0},

9.title: The people who were not a people
9.text: Quoting the prophet Hosea, Paul shows that God would call 'my people' those who were not his people — the Gentiles. The inclusion of the nations was already announced in the scriptures of Israel.

9.question: What did Isaiah prophesy about Israel, quoted by Paul?
options: Though they be as the sand of the sea, only the remnant shall be saved
That they will return from Egypt

9.template: Behold, I lay in {0} a stumblingstone

pairs.right: In whom Abraham's seed would be called
Chosen before birth, to show election
Image of God's sovereignty over man
Prophet quoted about the Gentiles as God's people

10.title: Christ is the end of the law
10.text: Israel had a zeal for God but sought its own righteousness through the works of the law. Paul declares that Christ is the end of the law: in him righteousness is reached by faith, not by the effort of keeping commandments.

10.template: For {0} is the end of the law for righteousness
if thou shalt confess with thy {0} the Lord Jesus,

10.question: What does Paul affirm about everyone who calls on the name of the Lord?

10.title: How beautiful are the feet
10.text: To show the need for preaching, Paul quotes Isaiah: 'How beautiful are the feet of them that preach the gospel of peace.' Faith comes by hearing, and hearing by the preached word of God.

10.question: What did Isaiah boldly say, quoted by Paul, about the Gentiles?
options: I was found of them that sought me not

pairs.right: The end of the law for the righteousness of the believer
With the mouth and the heart, unto salvation

pairs.left: Call on the name of the Lord
pairs.right: Whoever does so shall be saved
Comes by hearing the word of God

11.title: Elijah's seven thousand
11.text: To prove that God has not cast away Israel, Paul recalls Elijah, who thought himself the only faithful one. God answered that he had reserved seven thousand who had not bowed the knee to Baal — a sign that there is always a remnant by grace.

11.template: of the seed of Abraham, of the tribe of {0}.

11.question: What does Paul say remains in Israel, according to the election of grace?
What effect did Israel's fall have, according to Paul?
options: Salvation came to the Gentiles, to provoke Israel to jealousy

11.template: and if the {0} be holy, so are the branches.

11.title: The olive tree
11.text: Paul compares Israel to an olive tree: some natural branches were broken off through unbelief, and the Gentiles, a wild olive, were grafted in their place. The image teaches the Gentiles not to be arrogant, for it is the root that sustains them.

11.template: and thou, being a wild {0}, wert grafted in among them,

11.question: What mystery does Paul reveal about Israel?
options: Blindness in part happened to Israel, until the fulness of the Gentiles comes in

11.template: For the {0} and calling of God are without repentance.

11.title: The doxology
11.text: After three chapters reflecting on God's plans, Paul bursts into a hymn of praise to the depth of God's wisdom: 'Of him, and through him, and to him, are all things; to whom be glory for ever.'

11.question: How does Paul close this section on God's plans?
options: Of him, through him and to him are all things; to him be glory

pairs.right: Prophet for whom God reserved seven thousand faithful
What remained of Israel by grace
Israel, into which the Gentiles were grafted
Shall be saved, according to Paul

12.title: A living sacrifice
12.text: Paul opens the practical part of the letter by asking believers to offer their own bodies as a 'living sacrifice'. Instead of the animal sacrifices of the temple, true worship is a whole life dedicated to God.

12.template: that ye present your bodies a {0} sacrifice, holy, acceptable unto God,

12.question: What does Paul ask instead of conforming to this world?
options: To be transformed by the renewing of the mind
To keep the law of Moses

12.title: Given to hospitality
12.text: Among the marks of sincere love, Paul highlights sharing with the saints in their needs and practicing hospitality. Welcoming travelers and fellow believers was essential for the first Christian communities, scattered across the empire.

12.question: What does Paul exhort believers to do with those who rejoice and those who weep?
options: Rejoice with those who rejoice and weep with those who weep

12.question: What is written about vengeance, according to Paul?
options: Vengeance is mine; I will repay, saith the Lord
Vengeance belongs to the just man

13.title: Subjection to the authorities
13.text: Paul teaches that every soul should be subject to the authorities, for there is no authority but of God. Written under the Roman empire, it is one of the most discussed texts on the relationship between Christians and civil power.

13.question: What does Paul teach about the higher powers?
options: There is no authority but of God; those that exist are ordained by him
They are enemies of the faith

13.template: for he that loveth another hath fulfilled the {0}.

13.title: Love fulfills the law
13.text: Paul sums up the commandments — not to commit adultery, kill, steal or covet — in one word: 'Thou shalt love thy neighbour as thyself.' Love works no ill to a neighbor; therefore it is the fulfilling of the law.

13.question: In what commandment does Paul sum up the whole law?
options: Thou shalt love thy neighbour as thyself
Thou shalt have no other gods

13.template: and let us put on the armour of {0}.

14.title: The strong and the weak
14.text: In the church at Rome there were tensions between Jewish and Gentile Christians over eating certain foods and keeping certain days. Paul asks that no one judge or despise a brother because of these differences.

14.question: How should the one who eats all things and the one who eats only herbs treat each other?
options: Not despise or judge one another, for God has received them
The strong should exclude the weak

14.template: For whether we live, we live unto the {0};

14.question: Why does Paul say we should not judge our brother?
options: Because we shall all stand before the judgment seat of Christ

14.title: Put no stumblingblock before a brother
14.text: Instead of judging one another, Paul asks them to decide not to put a stumblingblock or occasion to fall before a brother. The freedom of the strong must not destroy the one for whom Christ died.

14.template: For the {0} of God is not meat and drink;

14.question: According to Paul, what is sin in relation to faith?

pairs.right: Should not judge each other over foods and days
Where each will give account of himself to God
Righteousness, peace and joy in the Holy Ghost
Is sin

15.title: Written for our learning
15.text: Paul affirms that whatever was written in earlier times was written for our learning, so that through the patience and comfort of the scriptures we might have hope. It is one of the great statements on the value of scripture.

15.template: comfort of the scriptures might have {0}.

15.question: What does Paul exhort believers to do to one another, as Christ did?
options: Receive one another, to the glory of God

15.title: The root of Jesse
15.text: Quoting Isaiah, Paul applies to the Gentiles the prophecy of a 'root of Jesse' who would rise to reign over the nations. Jesse was the father of David, and the prophecy pointed to the Messiah, in whom the Gentiles would trust.

15.template: There shall be a root of {0},

15.question: How does Paul describe his role among the Gentiles?
options: A minister of Jesus Christ, ministering the gospel of God

15.question: How far does Paul say he had preached the gospel of Christ?
options: From Jerusalem and round about unto Illyricum

15.title: The plan to reach Spain
15.text: Paul reveals his desire to preach where Christ had not yet been named, reaching as far as Spain — then the far west of the empire. He hoped to visit Rome on the way, but first he had to take the collection to Jerusalem.

15.question: Where did Paul plan to go, passing through Rome?

15.template: for the poor saints which are at {0}.

15.question: What does Paul ask the brethren of Rome regarding his trip to Jerusalem?
options: That they strive together with him in prayers to God for him
That they follow him to Jerusalem

pairs.right: Written for our learning and hope
Prophecy of the Messiah in whom Gentiles trust
Offering of the Gentiles for the saints of Jerusalem
Destination Paul desired to reach

16.title: Phebe, the letter-bearer
16.text: Paul commends Phebe, a servant (deaconess) of the church at Cenchrea, the port of Corinth. It is likely that she carried the letter to the Romans to Rome, entrusted with a document that would shape all of Christian theology.

16.question: Whom does Paul commend at the start of the chapter, calling her a servant of the church at Cenchrea?

16.title: The many greetings
16.text: This chapter contains the largest list of personal greetings in the New Testament, with more than twenty names — many of them women, such as Priscilla, Mary and Junia, singled out by Paul as important co-workers in the work.

16.question: How does Paul describe Andronicus and Junia?
options: His kinsmen and fellow-prisoners, of note among the apostles

16.question: With what gesture does Paul ask the brethren to greet one another?
What does Paul ask the brethren to beware of?
options: Those who cause divisions and offences against the doctrine learned

16.title: The closing doxology
16.text: Paul closes the letter with a doxology to him who can establish believers according to the gospel and the revelation of the mystery, now made manifest to all nations. 'To God only wise, be glory through Jesus Christ for ever.'

16.question: Who says he wrote the letter to the Romans at Paul's dictation?

pairs.right: Servant of Cenchrea commended by Paul
Co-workers who risked their lives for Paul
Wrote the letter at Paul's dictation
Closing in glory to the only wise God
